title: The Doctrine of Reflection (Pratibimbavāda) in the Tantrāloka
subtitle: Anupāya, self-luminosity of consciousness, the 50 phonemes, jīvanmukti, and rasa as spiritual cognition

summary.one_line: Abhinavagupta's exposition of the Doctrine of Reflection in Āhnikas 2-3 of the Tantrāloka: all phenomena are reflections in the mirror of Bhairava's consciousness.
summary.scope: Covers Anupāya (Chapter 2), the pratibimbavāda (Chapter 3), the fifty phonemes as forms of reflective awareness, jīvanmukti, and aesthetic-spiritual rasa.

sources.label: Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 2 (Āhnikas 2-3), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski

body.text: No Means (Anupāya) in the highest sense is Anuttara, the Absolute, itself. From that perspective, nothing has ever taken place — whether creation, destruction, bondage or liberation. This is total absence of practice, as there is none who practices, no goal and no way. A slightly lower perspective, which lasts just a timeless moment for those blessed with the most intense form of the power of grace, is a kind of 'letting go' in which nothing is abandoned and nothing is adopted. It is finally giving up all means to know and attain it, and just taking ultimate, eternal rest in the recognition of Anuttara, which cannot be attained by any means. (TĀv 2/2-7, pp. 2-15)

Śiva, who is self-luminous Light, shines by no means to realisation; rather, all means shine by His grace. 'I am He Himself who, self-luminous and the nature of all things, manifest all things.' Consciousness is self-luminous by nature and shines with its own light, not by that of another. If consciousness were not to be self-luminous, nothing at all would manifest. The Light of consciousness shines, self-luminous in the perceiver, and so illumines himself. Being pure in all its parts, and because it is self-luminous, consciousness is the most excellent medium of reflection. (TĀv 2/8-14, pp. 16-24)

The Doctrine of Reflection (pratibimbavāda) maintains that all that is objectively perceived, whether outer objects, sensory perceptions or inner cogitations, shines within consciousness at one with it as do reflections with the mirror in which they are reflected. But while the reflections in a mirror are those of an original object outside the mirror, these reflections are produced by the inherent power of the mirror of Bhairava's consciousness. The Buddhist idealists had made this a cornerstone of their idealism, but for them the world is just an unreal appearance. However, Abhinava, following Utpaladeva, takes care to stress that the reflections are real. (TĀv 3/1-4, pp. 62-70)

All this mass of phenomena is just a reflection in the Sky of Consciousness, because it possesses the characteristic mark of a reflection, namely, that it cannot manifest separately from the medium of reflection, and that it manifests mingled with other reflections. That is a reflection, like the form of a face in a mirror, like taste in saliva, smell in the nose, an erotic touch in the organ of touch, and an echo in the sky. All things are reflections within the Light of the Supreme Lord. The cause of reflection is the Supreme Lord's power, otherwise called freedom. (TĀv 3/24-34ab, pp. 99-108; TSā p. 10-12)

The alphabet of fifty forms of reflective awareness is expounded as the sequence of the arising of the letters from A to KṢ. The letter A is Anuttara and Anākhya, Kula and Akula, Unstruck Sound and Kaulikīśakti. The letter Ā is the union of Śiva and Śakti. The vowels I, Ī, U, Ū are the powers of will, sovereignty, and knowledge respectively. The letters of the alphabet are not merely linguistic signs but are the actual energies of consciousness — the fifty forms of its reflective awareness. The threefold emission as Śakti, Prāṇa and Parā Kuṇḍalinī pervades the entire matrix of the letters. (TĀv 3/65cd-197ab, pp. 136-314)

He who enters repeatedly into this state of penetration (samāveśa) into pure Bhairava consciousness free of thought constructs, attains Bhairava's state, otherwise called liberation in this life (jīvanmukti). The liberated soul's only purpose is to elevate others. The yogi free of thought constructs, beholding that Śiva also reflected in the noble and uncreated mirror of consciousness, becomes by himself spontaneously Bhairava. Liberation in this life takes place by an intense descent of the power of grace; it is not simply attained by 'being natural' or 'letting go.' Grace is essential, but so is the cooperation of the recipient. (TĀv 3/271cd-274ab, pp. 395-396)

Aesthetic experience is the arousal of the vitality of consciousness. This vitality is the source of life and so emission is all things. The aesthetic delight of Śiva's power of emission is the same rasa that is tasted in aesthetic experience — whether in music, poetry, or drama. Abhinava understands aesthetic rapture (rasa) as a form of spiritual cognition, a tasting of the bliss of one's own consciousness. The supreme triad and the groups of six, the all-embracing wheel of the twelve Kālikās — all are forms of the one aesthetic-spiritual consciousness that, when tasted as rasa, reveals the self-luminous nature of the perceiver. (TĀv 3/229-247ab, pp. 351-371)

The seal of the Supreme Means is the awareness that everything is reflected in the mirror of consciousness. 'I am the author of the cycle of creation and destruction that arises in the mirror of my consciousness.' The Śāmbhava master is one who abides in this realization continuously. The Supreme Means is beyond ritual. The reflection of the principles (tattva) in the fifty forms of reflective awareness is the means by which the yogi, beholding each principle — from Earth up to Śiva — reflected within his own nature by knowledge free of thought constructs, directly experiences it as pure consciousness and assumes Bhairava's nature. (TĀv 3/268-293ab, pp. 393-411)

The progressive emergence or 'articulation' of the energies of the reflective awareness, symbolized by the letters of the alphabet, was described previously by Abhinava. From the perspective of the Trika of the PT, elaborated extensively by Abhinava, the letter A stands for Anuttara and is Akula. Its power is the unfolding perception of the totality of all things in their fundamental nature as the flow of the energies of pure self-awareness i.e. AHAṀ ('I') of Bhairava/Śiva, the supreme perceiver. It encompasses the energies of the letters from A to H, that are as many forms of reflective awareness (parāmarśa), that extend through, even as they produce, the reality levels (tattva) from Earth to Śiva in a perpetual cycle. (TĀv 3/65cd-197ab, pp. 136-314)

To sum up: there are three levels of emission (visarga), which are identified in this passage of the Triśirobhairava with three forms of Kuṇḍalinī. 1) Parā Kuṇḍalinī is the goddess Parā, who is Kaulikīśakti, identified with the Nectar which is the seventeenth energy of the Moon. She is pure consciousness alone at rest in its own nature. 2) Prāṇa Kuṇḍalinī. This is the outpouring of the intermediate (parāpara) form of emission as the intention to make all things manifest. It is the level in which the first transformation of consciousness arises as the vital breath. It is represented by visarga (H). 3) Śakti Kuṇḍalinī is the lower form, in which there is no emission of higher states and Kuṇḍalinī rests in herself alone. In this way, the play of emanation of lower and higher states and their withdrawal is the flow of emission (visarga) brought about by the union of transcendental Śiva and immanent Śakti. (TĀv 3/138-141, pp. 243-251)

Abhinava supplies the following concise summary of the Doctrine of Reflection (pratibimbavāda) in his Tantrasāra: 'All this mass of phenomena is just a reflection in the Sky of Consciousness, because it possesses the characteristic mark of a reflection. This is the characteristic mark of a reflection, namely, that it cannot manifest separately (from the medium of reflection), and that it manifests mingled with other (reflections). That is a reflection, like the form of a face in a mirror, like taste in saliva, smell in the nose, an erotic touch in the organ of touch, and an echo in the sky. All things are reflections within the Light of the Supreme Lord. The cause of reflection is the Supreme Lord's power, otherwise called freedom.' (TĀv 3/24-34ab, pp. 99-108; TSā p. 10-12)

Aesthetic experience is the arousal of the vitality of consciousness. This vitality is the source of life and so emission is all things. The aesthetic delight of Śiva's power of emission is the same rasa that is tasted in aesthetic experience — whether in music, poetry, or drama. Abhinava understands aesthetic rapture (rasa) as a form of spiritual cognition, a tasting of the bliss of one's own consciousness. The supreme triad and the groups of six, the all-embracing wheel of the twelve Kālikās — all are forms of the one aesthetic-spiritual consciousness that, when tasted as rasa, reveals the self-luminous nature of the perceiver. (TĀv 3/229-247ab, pp. 351-371)

He who enters repeatedly into this state of penetration (samāveśa) into pure Bhairava consciousness free of thought constructs, attains Bhairava's state, otherwise called liberation in this life (jīvanmukti). The liberated soul's only purpose is to elevate others. The yogi free of thought constructs, beholding that Śiva also reflected in the noble and uncreated (akalpita) mirror of consciousness, becomes by himself spontaneously Bhairava. If the Goddess of Consciousness, who is the Centre, expands as the inner nature of all things by the process of the practice taught, or if by the process that will be taught next, Brahmā's Channel, which is the Centre, expands, then by virtue of that expansion, one attains the bliss of consciousness. And so he attains the liberation in this life taught previously. (TĀv 3/271cd-274ab, pp. 395-396)

gaps: Full verse-by-verse treatment of the fifty phonemes needs expansion from the detailed commentary

issues.title: Fifty phoneme section drawn from summary only
issues.description: The exposition of the fifty phonemes is vast; only a summary passage is included. Detailed passages on individual phoneme groups would strengthen the RO.

issues.title: Detailed exposition of individual phoneme groups still needed
issues.description: While the overview of the alphabet is now present, detailed passages on individual phoneme groups (vowels, consonants, semivowels, sibilants) would strengthen the RO.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Tantrāloka Volume 2, Āhnikas 2-3. Covers Anupāya, Pratibimbavāda, fifty phonemes, jīvanmukti, and rasa.

Expanded with 5 additional passages: yoga of reflection on the alphabet, threefold emission as Śakti/Prāṇa/Parā Kuṇḍalinī, the mirror teaching, aesthetic experience as consciousness-vitality, and jīvanmukti.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Tantrāloka Volume 2
Expanded with passages on alphabet reflection, threefold emission, mirror teaching, rasa, and jīvanmukti
